description: The current image is extremely blurry and appears to be a corrupted placeholder, with no readable content related to the research on low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC (x = 0.5, 0.8, 1.2 wt%) steels. No identifiable microstructure, mechanical property curve, characterization spectrum, or other experimental/analysis results can be extracted from the available visual data, so no evidence-based scientific conclusions can be drawn from this image alone. Detailed information about the tensile deformation behavior, phase composition, strain hardening characteristics, and mechanical performance of the studied steels is only recorded in the provided text extraction content, which cannot be verified via the current visual material.

This figure shows optical microstructures of three low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels with different carbon contents, all observed at the same magnification with a 40 μm scale bar. Subfigure (a) presents the microstructure of 0.5C steel, which exhibits a banded duplex ferritic-austenitic structure with ~40.8% ferrite and ~59.2% austenite. Subfigure (b) shows the 0.8C steel, which also has a duplex structure but with a higher austenite fraction (~84.8%) and finer, less elongated phase distribution. Subfigure (c) displays the 1.2C steel, which possesses a fully austenitic microstructure with visible annealing twins inside grains. A clear trend is observed: increasing carbon content reduces ferrite content, elevates austenite fraction, and eventually eliminates ferrite to form single-phase austenite at 1.2 wt% C. These results demonstrate that carbon concentration effectively modulates the phase constitution and microstructure of the Fe–Mn–Al–C steel system, laying the structural basis for their distinct tensile deformation behaviors and mechanical properties.

This group of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images with corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns exhibit the microstructures of low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels with varied carbon contents, with scale bars of 0.2 μm or 0.5 μm. Subfigures (a) and (b) belong to 0.5C steel: (a) shows the austenite (γ) phase, (b) presents the ferrite (α) phase. Subfigures (c) to (e) belong to 0.8C steel: (c) displays γ phase and κ carbide, (d) shows twins within γ phase, (e) is the dark-field image of α phase. Subfigure (f) is the dark-field image of γ phase in 1.2C steel. SAED indexing confirms that 0.8C steel contains κ carbide ((Fe,Mn)₃AlCₓ) in austenite, plus B2 domains with fine DO3 particles in ferrite, while 1.2C steel contains κ carbides in its austenite matrix. These results verify that increasing C content facilitates austenite formation and κ carbide precipitation, modifies the phase composition of the steels, and provides microstructural evidence for interpreting their distinct tensile deformation properties.

This figure presents the engineering stress-strain curves of low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels with three different carbon contents: 0.5 wt% C (black curve, labeled 0.5C), 0.8 wt% C (red curve, labeled 0.8C), and 1.2 wt% C (green curve, labeled 1.2C). The x-axis represents engineering strain, while the y-axis represents engineering stress in units of MPa. As observed, as carbon content increases from 0.5 wt% to 1.2 wt%, the total elongation of the steels rises continuously, reaching a maximum of 56.2% for the 1.2C sample. The ultimate tensile strength first increases to a peak value of 979 MPa for the 0.8C sample, then slightly reduces to 947 MPa for the 1.2C sample. The 1.2C steel shows the most excellent strength-ductility combination, with its strength-ductility product reaching 53.2 GPa%, nearly twice that of the 0.5C steel, verifying the remarkable influence of carbon content on the tensile performance of this low-density steel system.

This figure plots the strain hardening rate (unit: MPa) as a function of true strain for three low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels, with x = 0.5 (black curve, 0.5C steel), 0.8 (red curve, 0.8C steel), and 1.2 (green curve, 1.2C steel) wt.% carbon. All three samples present an initial strain hardening rate close to 4000 MPa at very low strain. The 0.5C and 0.8C steels show continuously decreasing strain hardening rates with rising strain, dropping to 0 at ~0.27 and ~0.32 strain, respectively. In comparison, the 1.2C steel exhibits three-stage strain hardening: after an initial sharp decrease to ~1650 MPa at ~0.05 strain, its strain hardening rate recovers to a plateau of ~1900 MPa, and only declines sharply to 0 at ~0.45 strain. The results verify that higher carbon content extends the strain hardening capacity of the steels, corresponding to better ductility, and the distinct strain hardening behaviors are associated with their different microstructures and deformation mechanisms.

This figure presents Crussard–Jaoul (C-J) analysis plots of ln(dσ/dε) versus lnε for low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels with varied carbon contents. Subplot (a) for 0.5C steel shows two-stage strain hardening with a transition critical strain ε=0.025. Subplot (b) for 0.8C steel also displays two-stage strain hardening with a transition critical strain ε=0.022, and its first-stage slope is steeper than that of 0.5C steel, which is ascribed to the existence of κ carbides in the austenite matrix. Subplot (c) for 1.2C steel exhibits three-stage strain hardening, with two transition critical strains at ε=0.026 and ε=0.137. The C-J analysis matches well with the experimental strain hardening behaviors of the steels, which are consistent with their microstructure evolution during tensile deformation, verifying that carbon content and precipitated phases significantly regulate the strain hardening characteristics of Fe–Mn–Al–C steels.

This set of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images presents the microstructural evolution of Fe–18Mn–10Al–0.5C duplex ferrite-austenite low-density steel during room temperature tensile deformation. Subfigures (a) and (b) correspond to the early deformation stage at true strain ε=0.02: (a) shows planar slip traces in austenite (γ) phase with a 0.2 μm scale bar, (b) exhibits dislocation tangles in ferrite (α) phase with a 0.2 μm scale bar. Subfigures (c) to (f) correspond to the later deformation stage at true strain ε=0.18: (c) shows intensive dislocation slip in γ (0.2 μm scale bar), (d) presents Taylor lattice structure in γ (0.5 μm scale bar), (e) displays deformation microbands in γ (0.2 μm scale bar), (f) reveals dense dislocation tangles in α (0.2 μm scale bar). The observed microstructural evolution matches the two-stage strain hardening behavior of the 0.5C steel: early planar slip and initial dislocation tangles correspond to the first strain hardening stage, while later developed Taylor lattice, microbands and higher-density dislocation tangles contribute to the relatively high strain hardening in the second stage, providing direct microscopic evidence for the tensile deformation mechanism of the studied steel.
